Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 75 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 2.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking the circle moved orange to (15,50), not (12,49) as I expected. Also, the circle toggled to white (Target W at 18,57).
More importantly, I see a new gray target at (18,46)! This is where the orange square was previously. So when the orange square leaves a position, it leaves behind a gray endpoint marker.
Also, the orange square is now at (15,50), which is close to the gray endpoint at (9,49).
Let me also note: the diamond is no longer listed as a target. Looking at the image, the diamond at (51,40) is still orange. So it wasn't toggled.
Wait, actually the diamond WAS toggled earlier. Let me check the image... The diamond at (51,40) is orange in the image. So it's back to orange.
Hmm, but I didn't click the diamond recently. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, I see the diamond is orange (O). And the circle is white (W) at (18,57).
Now, the orange square is at (15,50). Let me try clicking it to move it closer to (9,49).
Actually, wait. I also notice something: there's a gray target at (23,43). But earlier, when the orange square visited (23,43), it turned into a white star. Now it's gray again! This means the activation is not permanent - it resets when the square leaves.
So the gray endpoints are:
- (9,49) - gray
- (18,46) - gray (newly appeared)
- (23,43) - gray (was activated, now reset)
- (48,54) - gray
And the circle is white at (18,57).
Let me think about this differently. The orange square moves along the white path. The white path has several positions:
- (23,43) - junction
- (18,46) - intermediate
- (15,50) - current position
- (9,49) - endpoint
- (11,42) - endpoint (not on current path segment)
When the orange square visits a position and leaves, that position becomes a gray endpoint.
So the gray endpoints are positions the orange square has visited. The goal might be to visit ALL positions on the white path with the orange square, and ALL positions on the gray path with the purple square.
Currently, the orange square has visited: (23,43), (18,46), (12,44), (15,50). The remaining positions to visit might be (9,49) and (11,42).
And the purple square needs to visit positions on the gray path, including (48,54).
But I still can't move the purple square! Let me think about how to do that.
Actually, let me re-examine the early game. In the early game, clicking squares moved the purple square. What was different?
In the early game:
- Diamond was orange (untoggled)
- Circle was purple (untoggled)
- Orange square was at (23,43)
After I started toggling targets:
- Diamond became white, then back to orange
- Circle became white, then back to purple, then white again
Currently:
- Diamond is orange
- Circle is white
So the circle is white. Maybe when the circle is white, clicking squares moves the orange square. And when the circle is purple, clicking squares moves the purple square.
Let me test this by clicking the circle to toggle it to purple, then clicking the purple square to see if it moves.
Wait, but I tried this earlier and it didn't work. Let me re-examine...
Actually, I'm not sure if the circle was truly purple when I tested. The circle toggles between purple and white each time I click it. Currently it's white. If I click it, it should become purple.
Let me try: click circle (to make it purple), then click purple square.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Circle is now white, orange at (15,50). I notice gray endpoints appear where orange has visited. Let me try toggling the circle back to purple, then clicking the purple square to see if that enables moving purple.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click circle to toggle to purple
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 18, 'col': 57}])
print(f"Click circle: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        cr = sum(x[0] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(x[1] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Orange square: ({cr:.0f},{cc:.0f})")
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        cr = sum(x[0] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(x[1] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Purple square: ({cr:.0f},{cc:.0f})")
    if n['pixels'] == 12:
        cr = sum(x[0] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(x[1] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Target {n['color']} at ({cr:.0f},{cc:.0f})")

# Now click purple square to see if it moves
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 48}])
print(f"\nClick purple: done={result2.get('done')}, level_completed={result2.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        cr = sum(x[0] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(x[1] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Orange square: ({cr:.0f},{cc:.0f})")
    if n['color'] == 'p' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        cr = sum(x[0] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        cc = sum(x[1] for x in n['boundary']) / len(n['boundary'])
        print(f"Purple square: ({cr:.0f},{cc:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
